trajectory_summary: The call moves from neutral to conditionally booked, but the booking is structurally fragile. Dave's core anxiety — ROI at 73 on a potentially large cash purchase — is never resolved; it's deferred to the closer with no explicit bridge. The rep completes the procedural beats (verify, bill collection, appointment set) but misses every emotional anchor opportunity, calls Dave by the wrong name, and books without confirming the son (co-title holder) will be present.
outcome_dispute: The call ends with a booked phone appointment for the following day at noon. However, the booking is conditional: Dave accepted only after rejecting the in-home format, his cost anxiety was not resolved, his son (co-title holder) was not included, and he was called 'Gabe' rather than 'Dave' in the closing stretch. The appointment is real but fragile — closer should treat this as a warm lead with unresolved ROI anxiety, not a clean sit.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Vulnerability disclosure + cost-anxiety routing to specialist
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'm 73 years old. So if I gotta put a bunch of money in it, then it wasn't worth it to me to say so.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Dave's age-and-ROI disclosure is the emotional core of the call and the rep misreads it as a financing objection — twice. This is the moment that determines whether the closer walks into a warm sit or a skeptical one. The rep needs to learn to distinguish 'I can't afford it' (financing frame) from 'Is this worth it at my stage of life?' (life-stage frame) and route the latter to the specialist rather than answering it herself. This pattern will recur on every older-demographic call on this floor.

key_moments.quote: I have an excellent credit score. But why would I need credit?
key_moments.stewart_read: Dave asks a legitimate question about why credit matters for solar. The rep's answer — 'I honestly don't know. I'm kind of on the bottom of the food chain here' — is transparent but undermines credibility at a moment when Dave is already in a neutral-to-skeptical posture. The strategic read would be that honesty here builds trust; the reactive read is that the rep had no prepared bridge to the specialist. The reactive read holds: the rep doesn't redirect to the specialist or offer a satisfying frame, she just admits ignorance and moves on.

key_moments.quote: Is there an upfront cost, or what's what's the deal? I don't wanna waste your time or mine.
key_moments.stewart_read: Dave's 'I don't wanna waste your time or mine' is a soft refusal — he's testing whether to stay on the call. The codex names this pattern explicitly: polite guilt framing is a soft exit, not a genuine cost question. The rep responds with a bill-swap explanation that Dave immediately says he doesn't follow. The flip opportunity here was to reframe Dave's framing: the cost of staying with CNT is the real waste. Instead the rep explains the product.

key_moments.quote: So now we we need to get the full twelve months of usage history. There's actually a bar graph on the bill. Are you seeing that, Ewers? K. That's what I need to see. So if you can take a photo of that and then text that bar graph to me.
key_moments.stewart_read: The Android photo-sharing sequence runs from approximately 07:06 to 09:55 — roughly three minutes of navigating Dave through his phone's share menu. The codex's elderly_low_tech_protocol is explicit: pivot to verbal readback within 2 minutes of friction, not 10. Dave had already offered to read the bill aloud at 06:13 ('You know what? Yes. It's 929.') — the rep had a verbal readback path available and chose the photo path instead. The photo path eventually succeeded, but the time burn and friction were avoidable.

key_moments.quote: I actually got two electric bills. I got a I got a pool house for my swimming pool too. So — Oh, okay. The two electric meters. What are you looking for?
key_moments.stewart_read: Dave discloses a pool house with a separate electric meter. This is a secondary structure — a potential cross-sell routing signal. The rep asks whether Dave wants to combine the two meters, Dave declines ('It don't matter. I just no. I don't.'), and the rep moves on. The pool house is never flagged for the closer or routed as a cross-sell signal. A pool also implies higher seasonal electric load, which strengthens the bill-pain anchor the rep never fully deployed.
